The case is the most direct real-time test of dual-alignment swing-state viability in the current great-power competition.
similarities: Tiny state with geographic chokepoint leverage hosting both US and Chinese physical infrastructure simultaneously, extracting rent from both — the Gulf compute pole's exact logic applied to military rather than digital infrastructure
Both great powers have accepted the other's physical presence in the same territory rather than forcing a binary choice, because the chokepoint value outweighs the cost of tolerating the rival
The host country uses competing great-power bids to ratchet up rental terms — exactly the commercial leverage model Gulf AI hubs are attempting
Illustrates that dual-alignment is currently operative, not merely theoretical, in the US-China competition
differences: Djibouti's debt exposure to China (~70%+ of GDP) signals that the leverage balance has already shifted: it is closer to a Chinese debt-dependent client than a true neutral swing state — the critical structural warning for the Gulf compute pole
Military bases involve physical troops and coercive potential that commercial data-center relationships do not; the coercion gradient is much steeper
Djibouti is extremely capital-poor; its leverage is fragile, whereas Gulf states' sovereign-wealth-fund capital surplus is structurally different — this is the key distinguishing variable
Djibouti's 'swing' is geographically constrained (one chokepoint); Gulf states can replicate compute infrastructure across multiple jurisdictions, giving them more exit options
analogies.implication: Djibouti is the live stress-test of dual-alignment swing-state strategy and reveals the critical failure mode: infrastructure debt taken from one bloc in exchange for letting it build presence gradually transfers leverage from the host to the creditor. Gulf states' capital surplus inverts this dynamic — they are the capital exporters, not importers — which is the structural reason their swing-state position is far more durable than Djibouti's. The key risk to watch is whether 'free' infrastructure offers (Chinese AI cloud deals, US chip-access arrangements) carry embedded dependencies that replicate the Djibouti debt dynamic in digital form.

analogies.case: The Bank for International Settlements as neutral financial infrastructure serving both Allied and Axis belligerents, 1930–1945
analogies.what_happened: The BIS was created in Basel in 1930 under the Young Plan to manage German reparations, with treaty-based sovereign immunity and a board spanning all major industrial powers. When WWII began, it continued to operate with board members from Germany, Britain, France, Italy, Japan, and (until Pearl Harbor) the US. American president Thomas McKittrick ran it through the war. The BIS conducted gold transactions for the Reichsbank — including gold looted from occupied central banks and Holocaust victims — while simultaneously serving Allied central banks. Allied delegates used BIS channels to maintain intelligence on German gold reserves. The Bretton Woods Conference (1944) passed a Norwegian/US resolution calling for the BIS's dissolution due to Nazi-gold collaboration. That resolution was never implemented: post-war, the BIS became more important than ever, hosting the Basel process for bank capital standards, becoming the 'central bankers' bank.'
similarities: Neutral infrastructure chartered with sovereign immunity that continues to function specifically because disrupting it would hurt all sides equally — the structural logic proposed for neutral AI governance or 'cloud neutrality' frameworks
Located in and protected by a small, constitutionally neutral country (Switzerland) — directly parallels proposals for Gulf-hosted neutral AI compute infrastructure with treaty protections
The neutral infrastructure is the critical settlement layer: both blocs needed it for financial continuity, creating a mutual-deterrence logic against shutdown

Post-conflict, the 'neutral' infrastructure survived and became more systemically important — the BIS is a stronger institution today than in 1944, suggesting neutral infrastructure can achieve durable legitimacy
differences: The BIS's WWII 'neutrality' was morally catastrophic: it actively facilitated Nazi looted-gold transactions under cover of institutional immunity — neutral infrastructure can be weaponised by bad actors precisely because all sides are reluctant to shut it down

The BIS was created by the great powers themselves (bottom-up intergovernmental treaty), whereas Gulf compute infrastructure is commercially driven; the governance structures are fundamentally different

Bretton Woods attempted to abolish the BIS specifically for its wartime conduct; only US strategic interest in post-war financial coordination saved it — survival of neutral infrastructure depends on at least one great power finding it useful

The BIS held paper assets (gold, balances); physical compute infrastructure has real-world operational dependencies (chip supply chains, electricity, cooling) that make true neutrality harder to maintain under sanctions pressure
analogies.implication: The BIS case is the clearest historical precedent for neutral infrastructure with treaty-based sovereign immunity surviving a hot great-power conflict by being indispensable to all sides. It provides a template — and a warning — for Gulf compute neutrality: the governance architecture must be codified before conflict escalates, not improvised during it. The Nazi-gold scandal warns that 'neutral compute infrastructure' hosting authoritarian-state AI development faces an analogous legitimacy crisis: the immunity that makes it useful to all sides also makes it a conduit for the most problematic users.

analogies.case: Petrodollar recycling system — US-Saudi deal, 1974–present
analogies.what_happened: After the 1973 oil shock, Kissinger negotiated an arrangement with Saudi Arabia (extended to the broader GCC) in which Gulf states would price oil exclusively in US dollars and recycle surplus petrodollars into US Treasuries and financial markets. In exchange the US provided security guarantees and arms. This transformed the relationship from adversarial (1973 embargo) to structural co-dependency. The arrangement has lasted ~50 years; Gulf sovereign wealth funds became deeply embedded in Western financial markets, creating mutual lock-in. The 'recycling' mechanism was the innovation: resource rents converted into structural integration with the dominant power's architecture rather than expended on autonomous development.
similarities: Gulf states converting resource rents into structural embedding in a great-power's architecture as a form of protection
Leverage extracted from control over a globally critical commodity — the Gulf compute pole repeats this with AI infrastructure
Mutual dependency engineered deliberately: US needs Gulf dollar recycling; Gulf needs US security umbrella
Small states with massive capital surpluses using financial/infrastructure integration as a substitute for hard military power
The new Gulf hyperscaler hosting deals follow the same template: capital outflows to US (and now Chinese) tech firms recycled as compute presence and sovereign AI access
differences: The petrodollar deal was exclusively US-facing and bilateral; the Gulf compute pole explicitly seeks multi-alignment (US AND Chinese hyperscalers simultaneously)
Oil priced in dollars created a zero-sum arrangement with one hegemon; AI compute can theoretically be purchased from multiple providers concurrently — the dependency is less concentrated

Petrodollar recycling was through fungible financial instruments (Treasuries, equities); AI infrastructure creates physical path dependencies (chip architectures, software stacks) that are not fungible
The 1974 deal was state-to-state and secret; compute investments are public, multi-party, and involve private corporations not governments
Financial integration is reversible (you can sell Treasuries); swapping out a hyperscaler data-center stack mid-operation is not
analogies.implication: The Gulf is likely attempting to replicate petrodollar recycling with 'compute recycling' — hosting US and Chinese hyperscalers as a new structural protection mechanism. But the analogy breaks at fungibility: unlike dollars, AI compute stacks (Nvidia CUDA vs. Huawei Ascend) are technically bifurcating. The Gulf may end up with two separate, incompatible digital infrastructures mirroring the two blocs — the opposite of non-alignment. The critical test is whether Gulf sovereign AI investments (G42, KAUST, Falcon/TII) accumulate enough indigenous capability to remain the pivot rather than merely the host.

analogies.case: The Venetian Republic as neutral commercial entrepôt and the Arsenal, c.1000–1797
analogies.what_happened: Venice maintained profitable trade with both the Islamic world (Egypt, Fatimids, Mamluks, Ottomans) and Christian Europe throughout its ~1,100-year existence — including during the Crusades, for which Venice was repeatedly condemned but never stopped. The Venetian Arsenal (founded 1104, rebuilt 1320) became the world's first assembly-line industrial facility, capable of fitting out a complete war galley per day by 1574, giving Venice proprietary naval technology that made it indispensable rather than replaceable. Venice extracted concessions from both blocs: Crusaders needed Venetian shipping and navigation; Muslims needed Venetian trading relationships and glass, spice, and financial intermediation. The Republic sustained this position for eight centuries before Napoleon ended it in 1797.
similarities: Neutral commercial hub deliberately positioned between two hostile civilisational blocs
Proprietary indigenous technology stack (the Arsenal / shipbuilding / glassmaking monopoly) that made Venice structurally indispensable rather than a substitutable pass-through
Commercial pragmatism explicitly overriding ideological bloc solidarity — Venice traded with 'infidels' during Crusades; Gulf states host both US and Chinese hyperscalers
Small state with no realistic option for military dominance choosing neutral entrepôt as its primary strategic posture
differences: Venice had full sovereign control over its territory and an independent military; Gulf states operate under the US security umbrella and cannot fully replicate this
The Arsenal's technology was offensive/military (war galleys); the Gulf compute pole's technology is primarily commercial — the leverage is asymmetric

Venice's neutrality was often hypocritical at scale (it shaped Crusader campaigns — notably the 1202 diversion to Constantinople — to its commercial advantage); Gulf states face similar credibility questions about whether their 'non-alignment' is genuine or merely fee-extracting
Venice eventually succumbed to the cost-disease of sustained great-power competition (Ottoman closure of eastern trade routes) — a warning about single-resource-dependency entrepôts
analogies.implication: The most durable form of digital non-alignment may require genuine proprietary technological capability — an 'Arsenal' — not just hosting others' infrastructure. Gulf sovereign AI research initiatives (Falcon/TII, G42's AIM) are early attempts, but the gap with frontier models is immense. Without this indigenous stack, the entrepôt role is permanently extractable: the great powers can always relocate compute capacity to friendlier hosts. The eight-century Venetian run suggests that entrepôt neutrality can be very durable when backed by genuine technology differentiation; it collapses when the differentiation disappears.

analogies.case: Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty as technology-access-for-dependency deal, 1968–present
analogies.what_happened: The NPT (1968) created a two-tier system: Nuclear Weapon States (US, USSR, UK, France, China) agreed not to transfer nuclear weapons and nominally to disarm; Non-Nuclear Weapon States agreed not to develop weapons and accepted IAEA inspection regimes; in exchange, NWS would share civilian nuclear technology (power reactors, isotope medicine). In practice: civilian nuclear transfers created profound technical and fuel-cycle dependency on supplier states; IAEA safeguards created ongoing surveillance infrastructure; the disarmament commitment was never honoured. States that refused the treaty (India, Pakistan, Israel, North Korea) developed nuclear weapons independently at enormous cost but achieved genuine autonomy. States that complied received civilian nuclear access but accepted permanent structural dependency and inspection leverage.
similarities: Great powers offer access to transformative, dual-use technology in exchange for forswearing indigenous development and accepting ongoing inspection/dependency
The 'civilian vs. military' distinction that legitimises technology transfer while constraining sovereign capability — exactly mirrored in 'commercial AI' vs. 'AI weapons' framing today
Non-aligned states face a structural choice: accept the terms and get access, or pay the full cost of going indigenous
The inspection/transparency regime (IAEA → cloud audits, export controls, CHIPS Act conditions) creates ongoing surveillance leverage for the technology supplier
differences: Nuclear weapons are genuinely existential and physically discrete; AI capability has no equivalent clean line between 'civilian' and 'military' use
The NPT had formal multilateral treaty architecture with legal obligations; AI compute access agreements are private contracts between states and corporations with no equivalent enforceability
Nuclear indigenisation required state-level resources and was binary (you either have a bomb or you don't); AI capability development is a continuous spectrum and diffuses through open-source models
The 'rogue proliferator' failure mode (North Korea) required decades of isolation the Gulf states cannot sustain — their economies are too integrated
analogies.implication: The AI 'NPT problem': accepting US or Chinese hyperscaler infrastructure may closely resemble the NPT bargain — access to transformative technology in exchange for structural dependency and ongoing surveillance (usage telemetry, export-control certification, hardware backdoor risk). Countries that try to go indigenous (UAE's Falcon, Saudi SDAIA, India's IndiaAI) bear enormous costs but edge toward genuine autonomy. The Gulf compute pole may be in the 'civilian nuclear deal' phase — getting access at the cost of inspection dependencies that eventually become politically constraining when the geopolitical blocs harden. The NPT's critical lesson: the compliance trap is easy to enter and very hard to exit.

analogies.case: Swedish armed neutrality and commercial dual-bloc technology exports — Ericsson, Saab, Bofors, 1814–2024
analogies.what_happened: Sweden maintained armed neutrality from 1814 (after the Napoleonic wars) until joining NATO in 2024. Unlike Austria's constitutionalised passive neutrality, Sweden backed its non-alignment with genuine military capability (~4% GDP defence spending at Cold War peak) and developed world-class indigenous defence industries: Bofors artillery (exported to India, used in conflicts against Pakistan and China), Ericsson telecommunications infrastructure (sold to Soviet bloc countries and the West simultaneously), Saab aircraft (sold across alignment categories). Sweden embedded itself in the supply chains of multiple competing blocs through commercial technology provisioning, making Swedish neutrality commercially valuable to all parties and therefore politically sustainable. It remained outside NATO and the Warsaw Pact throughout the entire Cold War.
similarities: Neutrality backed by credible indigenous capability, not just political declaration — the Gulf compute pole without indigenous AI capability is merely declaratory
Commercial technology provisioning to multiple competing blocs simultaneously as the mechanism that makes neutrality economically self-sustaining
Refusal to join either bloc while economically integrating with both
Technology exports as the commercial instrument that gives the neutral party structural leverage with all sides
differences: Sweden's neutrality was geographically grounded in northern Europe's balance; Gulf states are at a strategic crossroads between multiple theatres with much higher great-power attention
Sweden's technology was primarily military-industrial; Gulf compute pole is civilian-commercial — the leverage profile is different
Sweden had strong democratic institutions that provided domestic political constraints on commercial relationships with authoritarian buyers; Gulf states lack equivalent institutional checks

Most critically: Swedish neutrality collapsed in 2024 under a single sufficiently large external shock (Ukraine invasion → NATO entry). Decades of structural neutrality can unravel quickly under direct great-power pressure — the Gulf's digital non-alignment faces an analogous vulnerability
analogies.implication: Sweden's model shows that credible non-alignment requires indigenous technological capability, not just commercial hosting of others' infrastructure. Gulf states are currently closer to 'compute entrepôts' (hosting others' equipment) than to Sweden's 'compute arsenal' (producing and exporting capability). The Swedish collapse into NATO also provides the sharpest warning: 210 years of neutrality ended within 18 months of a sufficiently large bloc-pressure event. The Gulf's digital non-alignment may similarly survive until a US-China confrontation reaches a crisis threshold that forces binary choice — at which point the lack of genuine technological independence becomes decisive.

analogies.case: ITU vs. ICANN — WCIT-12 Dubai and the internet governance fracture, 1998–2016
analogies.what_happened: When the internet globalised in the 1990s, the US retained de facto control through IANA (Internet Assigned Numbers Authority), then ICANN (est. 1998), which managed domain names, IP addresses, and the root server system under a US Department of Commerce contract. At the 2012 World Conference on International Telecommunications (WCIT-12) in Dubai, Russia, China, Arab states, and a majority of the Global South pushed to transfer internet governance to the ITU (a UN body with one-state-one-vote structure). The US, EU, and allies rejected this; 89 countries signed the revised ITRs, 55 (mostly Western) refused. The split institutionalised digital non-alignment as a formal governance category. Post-Snowden (2013), Brazil organised NETmundial (2014) as a multi-stakeholder alternative. In 2016 the US relinquished formal IANA oversight — a partial concession that preserved the ICANN structure while removing the explicit US government role.
similarities: Developing and non-aligned countries using international institutional forums to assert alternatives to US-controlled digital infrastructure — exactly the current AI governance debate
The 'legitimate multilateralism vs. US hegemony' framing maps directly onto arguments for Gulf/Global South compute poles
Small states attempting to use collective institutional weight to gain leverage over infrastructure they don't physically control

The US tactical concession (IANA transition 2016) — symbolic de-Americanisation while preserving structural US influence — is the template for how US hyperscalers may make similar concessions to Gulf hosts
differences: WCIT-12 failed — the signing countries got nothing materially; the US maintained effective ICANN/IANA control. Institutional campaigns for digital non-alignment have a poor track record against physical infrastructure facts

Internet governance was about naming/routing (relatively abstract and costless to duplicate); AI compute poles are about physical data centres requiring massive capital — more concrete leverage but also more irreversible commitment
The ITU coalition was substantially driven by Russia and China seeking censorship legitimacy, not genuine non-alignment; the Gulf compute pole has clearer commercial motivation

The relevant lesson is that the *failure* of WCIT-12 accelerated the infrastructure-sovereignty response — countries stopped seeking governance reform and started building alternative physical stacks (BeiDou, Huawei, undersea cables)
analogies.implication: The ITU vs. ICANN battle establishes that digital non-alignment campaigns that rely on *institutional reform* (petitioning the hegemon's governing bodies) consistently fail; the winning strategy is building alternative physical/technical infrastructure. The Gulf compute pole is the post-WCIT strategic conclusion: stop arguing about governance, start building the hardware. The 2016 IANA transition also models the likely US hyperscaler response to Gulf leverage — cosmetic concessions (local incorporation, data residency promises, minority board seats) while preserving effective architectural control through software stacks, chip dependencies, and update channels.

analogies.case: East India Company factory-to-sovereignty progression, 1612–1858
analogies.what_happened: The English East India Company established 'factories' (fortified trading posts) in Mughal and Deccan port cities under licence from local rulers who valued the trade revenue: Surat (1612), Madras (1639), Bombay (1668), Calcutta (1690). The initial arrangement was purely commercial and mutually beneficial. Over time the factories acquired: private security forces (sepoys), then standing military capability, then tax-collection rights (the Diwani grant of Bengal, 1765), then territorial sovereignty. The full progression from welcomed commercial post to imperial control took ~200 years but accelerated dramatically after 1757 (Plassey). Host rulers who initially extracted rents from the EIC presence found themselves progressively unable to operate outside the Company's terms, as switching costs, debt dependencies, and sepoy armies made exit prohibitively costly.
similarities: Foreign commercial actors establish physical infrastructure presence ('factories'/'data centres') in host territories under host invitation, with host extracting revenue
Initial arrangement is genuinely mutually beneficial — trade revenue then; compute revenue, AI access, and sovereign AI capability now

The commercial actor gradually acquires capabilities not in the original bargain: EIC got military and tax rights; hyperscalers accumulate behavioural data, regulatory standard-setting influence, and operational dependencies

Host sovereignty over the infrastructure erodes progressively as switching costs accumulate — sepoy-trained armies and EIC debt created lock-in exactly as proprietary chip architectures and ML pipeline lock-in do today
differences: The EIC had explicit coercive military capacity and formal imperial ambitions backed by the British Crown; hyperscalers are commercial corporations without armies and subject to (at least nominal) rule of law
The EIC operated in a pre-Westphalian sovereignty environment without international investment treaties or UN frameworks; Gulf states have legal recourse the Mughals did not
The EIC progression took centuries under pre-modern conditions; digital infrastructure dependencies can accumulate in 5–10 years, compressing the decision window
Gulf states have US security guarantees and sovereign wealth funds as counterbalancing leverage; the Mughal successors had neither — making the Gulf less vulnerable to the full EIC progression
analogies.implication: The Gulf compute pole's most serious long-run risk is not a dramatic forced alignment with one bloc but a quiet accumulation of dependencies — data flows, operational standards, supply-chain lock-in, regulatory norm adoption — that progressively constrains sovereign digital agency without any single visible coercive act. The EIC pattern shows that the progression from 'invited commercial presence' to 'structural subordination' is gradual, hard to reverse, and politically invisible until it's too late to exit. The critical governance question is whether Gulf states build parallel indigenous capability (data governance laws, sovereign model weights, domestic chip programmes) fast enough to preserve the credible threat of exit — the one thing that kept EIC factory hosts in control in the early decades.
